Mountain pass in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan

The **Khyber Pass**[[a]](#cite_note-1) is a [mountain pass](/wiki/Mountain_pass "Mountain pass") in the [Khyber District](/wiki/Khyber_District "Khyber District") of the [Pakistani](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan") province of [Khyber Pakhtunkhwa](/wiki/Khyber_Pakhtunkhwa "Khyber Pakhtunkhwa"), along the [international border](/wiki/Durand_Line "Durand Line") with [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"). It connects the town of [Landi Kotal](/wiki/Landi_Kotal "Landi Kotal") to the [Valley of Peshawar](/wiki/Valley_of_Peshawar "Valley of Peshawar") at [Jamrud](/wiki/Jamrud "Jamrud") by traversing part of the [White Mountains](/wiki/White_Mountains,_Afghanistan "White Mountains, Afghanistan"). Since it was part of the ancient [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road"), it has been a vital [trade route](/wiki/Trade_route "Trade route") between [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") and the [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") and a [strategic](/wiki/Military_strategy "Military strategy") military [choke point](/wiki/Choke_point "Choke point") for various states that controlled it. The Khyber Pass is considered one of the most famous mountain passes in the world.[[1]](#cite_note-2)

## Geography and demography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Khyber_Pass&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography and demography")]

Along Asian Highway 1 ([AH1](/wiki/AH1 "AH1")), the summit of the pass at [Landi Kotal](/wiki/Landi_Kotal "Landi Kotal") lies 5 km (3 mi) inside Pakistan, descending 460 m (1,510 ft) into the Peshawar Valley at Jamrud, about 30 km (19 mi) from the Afghan border, traversing part of the [Spin Ghar](/wiki/Spin_Ghar "Spin Ghar") mountains.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) The pass starts at Kadam near Jamrud, 18 km (11 mi) west of Peshawar. The total length of the pass proper is 25 mi (40 km) from Jamrud to Landi Kotal, although the lesser section of the pass runs for additional 8 mi (13 km) westwards until the town of Lōya Daka, in Afghanistan.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) The height of the summit is 3,373 ft (1,028 m).[[3]](#cite_note-Gupta22-4)

To the north of the Khyber Pass lies the country of the Shalmani tribe and [Mullagori tribe](/wiki/Mullagori "Mullagori"). To the south is Afridi [Tirah](/wiki/Tirah "Tirah"), while the inhabitants of villages in the Pass itself are [Afridi](/wiki/Afridi_(Pashtun) "Afridi (Pashtun)") clansmen.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Throughout the centuries, [Pashtun](/wiki/Pashtun_people "Pashtun people") clans, particularly the Afridis and the Afghan [Shinwari](/wiki/Shinwari "Shinwari"), have regarded the Pass as their own preserve and have levied a toll on travellers for safe conduct. Since this has long been their main source of income, resistance to challenges to the Shinwari's authority has often been fierce.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Ancient history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Khyber_Pass&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Ancient history")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/The_Khyber_Pass_with_the_fortress_of_Alimusjid_-_lithograph_by_James_Rattray_-_1848_%282%29.jpg/250px-The_Khyber_Pass_with_the_fortress_of_Alimusjid_-_lithograph_by_James_Rattray_-_1848_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Khyber_Pass_with_the_fortress_of_Alimusjid_-_lithograph_by_James_Rattray_-_1848_(2).jpg)

The Khyber Pass with the fortress of [Ali Masjid](/wiki/Ali_Masjid "Ali Masjid") in 1848

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Khyber_chiefs_with_captain_tucker.jpg/250px-Khyber_chiefs_with_captain_tucker.jpg)](/wiki/File:Khyber_chiefs_with_captain_tucker.jpg)

Afghan chiefs and a British [political officer](/wiki/Political_officer_(British_Empire) "Political officer (British Empire)") posing at [Jamrud Fort](/wiki/Jamrud_Fort "Jamrud Fort") at the mouth of the Khyber Pass in 1878

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Elephant_battery_of_heavy_artillery_along_the_Khyber_Pass_at_Campbellpur_LCCN2004707363.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Elephant_battery_of_heavy_artillery_along_the_Khyber_Pass_at_Campbellpur_LCCN2004707363.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elephant_battery_of_heavy_artillery_along_the_Khyber_Pass_at_Campbellpur_LCCN2004707363.tif)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Ford_Model_91A_at_Torkham_-_Annemarie_Schwarzenbach.jpg/250px-Ford_Model_91A_at_Torkham_-_Annemarie_Schwarzenbach.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ford_Model_91A_at_Torkham_-_Annemarie_Schwarzenbach.jpg)

Torkham in 1939

A number of historical invasions of the Indian subcontinent have been through the Khyber Pass, such as those of [Darius I](/wiki/Darius_the_Great "Darius the Great"), [Ardashir I](/wiki/Ardashir_I "Ardashir I"), [Shapur I](/wiki/Shapur_I "Shapur I") and [Anushirvan](/wiki/Khosrow_I "Khosrow I"),[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) and later, Mongols such as [Duwa](/wiki/Duwa "Duwa"), [Qutlugh Khwaja](/wiki/Qutlugh_Khwaja "Qutlugh Khwaja") and [Kebek](/wiki/Kebek "Kebek"). Prior to the [Kushan era](/wiki/Kushan_Empire "Kushan Empire"), the Khyber Pass was not a widely used trade route.[[4]](#cite_note-5)

The pass has been traversed by military expeditions launched by empires such as the Achaemenids and Sassanids, as well as by nomadic invaders from Central Asia, including the Saka, Yuezhi, and White Huns.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) Indian empires rarely extended their control beyond the pass, with the Maurya king [Chandragupta](/wiki/Chandragupta_Maurya "Chandragupta Maurya") being an exception.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3)

The Khyber Pass has witnessed the spread of Greek influence into India and the expansion of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") in the opposite direction.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) Despite military activities, trade continued to thrive there.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) The Khyber Pass became a critical part of the [Silk Road](/wiki/Silk_Road "Silk Road"), a major trade route from East Asia to Europe.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

The [Parthian Empire](/wiki/Parthian_Empire "Parthian Empire") fought for control of passes such as this to profit from the trade in silk, jade, rhubarb, and other luxuries moving from China to Western Asia and Europe. Through the Khyber Pass, [Gandhara](/wiki/Gandhara "Gandhara") (in present-day Pakistan) became a regional center of trade connecting [Bagram](/wiki/Bagram "Bagram") in Afghanistan to [Taxila](/wiki/Taxila "Taxila") in India, adding Indian luxury goods such as ivory, pepper, and textiles to the Silk Road commerce.[[6]](#cite_note-Docherty2008-7): 74

## Islamic period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Khyber_Pass&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Islamic period")]

During the Islamic period, Muslim rulers, including [Mahmud Ghaznavi](/wiki/Mahmud_Ghaznavi "Mahmud Ghaznavi"), [Muhammad of Ghor](/wiki/Muhammad_of_Ghor "Muhammad of Ghor"), [Timur](/wiki/Timur "Timur"), [Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur"), and [Nader Shah](/wiki/Nader_Shah "Nader Shah") used the Khyber and nearby passes for their invasions of the Indian subcontinent.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) The Mughals attempted to control the pass but faced resistance from local tribes.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) [Ahmad Shah Durrani](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_Durrani "Ahmad Shah Durrani") was the last major Islamic conqueror to cross the pass, though his successors' campaigns had limited lasting impact.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3)

## British rule

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Khyber_Pass&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: British rule")]

In the 19th century, the British [East India Company](/wiki/East_India_Company "East India Company") (EIC) aimed to secure the Khyber Pass from Russia as part of the [Great Game](/wiki/Great_Game "Great Game").[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3)

The region was contested during the [Anglo-Afghan Wars](/wiki/Anglo-Afghan_Wars "Anglo-Afghan Wars"), with control shifting between the British, Sikhs and Afghans.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikh_Empire "Sikh Empire") under [Ranjit Singh](/wiki/Ranjit_Singh "Ranjit Singh") captured the Khyber Pass in 1837, with the Sikh general [Hari Singh Nalwa](/wiki/Hari_Singh_Nalwa "Hari Singh Nalwa") constructing the [Jamrud Fort](/wiki/Jamrud_Fort "Jamrud Fort") to safeguard the pass.[[6]](#cite_note-Docherty2008-7) After the [Second Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Afghan_War "Second Anglo-Afghan War") ended in 1880, the Khyber region came under British control, and the policy of paying local tribes to maintain the route's security was implemented.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3)

The British invested in infrastructure development, building roads, railways, and telegraph lines through the pass.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) For strategic reasons, after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the government of [British India](/wiki/British_Raj "British Raj") built the heavily engineered [Khyber Pass Railway](/wiki/Khyber_Pass_Railway "Khyber Pass Railway"), from Jamrud, near Peshawar, to the Afghan border near Landi Kotal. It opened in 1925.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3) A common phrase during British colonial period described the length of what was then British India as "Khyber to [Kanyakumari](/wiki/Kanyakumari "Kanyakumari")".[[7]](#cite_note-Nalwa2009-8)[[8]](#cite_note-Rajghatta2017-9)

During [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), concrete [dragon's teeth](/wiki/Dragon%27s_teeth_(fortification) "Dragon's teeth (fortification)") were erected on the valley floor due to British fears of an invasion of India by the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers").[[9]](#cite_note-10)

## Post-1947

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Khyber_Pass&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Post-1947")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Khyber_pass.jpeg/250px-Khyber_pass.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Khyber_pass.jpeg)

[Bab-e-Khyber](/wiki/Bab-e-Khyber "Bab-e-Khyber"), the entrance gate of the Khyber Pass

Following the [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India") in 1947, the Khyber Pass became part of Pakistan. Passenger services through the pass have been intermittent, with the Khyber Steam Safari, a joint venture between a private company and [Pakistan Railways](/wiki/Pakistan_Railways "Pakistan Railways"), operating in the 1990s.[[2]](#cite_note-auto-3)

The Pass became widely known to thousands of Westerners and Japanese who traveled it in the days of the [hippie trail](/wiki/Hippie_trail "Hippie trail"), taking a bus or car from Kabul to the Afghan border. At the Pakistani frontier post, travellers were advised not to wander away from the road, as the location was a barely controlled [Federally Administered Tribal Area](/wiki/Federally_Administered_Tribal_Areas "Federally Administered Tribal Areas"). Then, after customs formalities, a quick daylight drive through the Pass was made. Monuments left by [British Indian Army](/wiki/British_Indian_Army "British Indian Army") units from the era of British colonial rule, as well as hillside forts, could be viewed from the highway.

The area of the Khyber Pass has been connected with a counterfeit arms industry that makes various types of weapons known to gun collectors as [Khyber Pass copies](/wiki/Khyber_Pass_copy "Khyber Pass copy") using local steel and [blacksmiths'](/wiki/Blacksmith "Blacksmith") [forges](/wiki/Forge "Forge").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/KhyberRailway_02.jpg/250px-KhyberRailway_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:KhyberRailway_02.jpg)

The pass was serviced by the [Khyber Pass Railway](/wiki/Khyber_Pass_Railway "Khyber Pass Railway"), currently closed.

During the [War in Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%93present) "War in Afghanistan (2001–present)"), the Khyber Pass was a major route for resupplying military armament and food to [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") forces in the Afghan theater of conflict since the beginning of the invasion in 2001. Almost 80% of the NATO and US supplies that were brought in by road were transported through the Khyber Pass. It was also used to transport civilians from the Afghan side to the Pakistani one. Until the end of 2007, the route had been relatively safe, since the tribes living there (mainly the [Afridi](/wiki/Afridi "Afridi"), a [Pashtun tribe](/wiki/Pashtun_tribe "Pashtun tribe")) were paid by the Pakistani government to keep the area safe.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

In January 2009, Pakistan sealed off the bridge as part of a military offensive against Taliban guerrillas. This military operation was mainly focused on Jamrud, a district on the Khyber road. The target was to “dynamite or bulldoze homes belonging to men suspected of harboring or supporting Taliban militants or carrying out other illegal activities”.[[10]](#cite_note-11)

This increasingly unstable situation in northwest Pakistan, where the US accused Pakistan of hosting the Taliban, made the US and NATO broaden supply routes, through Central Asia (Turkmenistan, Uzbekistan and Tajikistan). Even the option of supplying material through the Iranian far southeastern port of [Chabahar](/wiki/Chabahar "Chabahar") was considered.[[11]](#cite_note-12)

In 2010 NATO forces executed a helicopter attack across the border [between Afghanistan and Pakistan](/wiki/Durand_Line "Durand Line") and killed three Pakistani soldiers in an airstrike.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-13) In response, Pakistan closed the pass to NATO supply trucks, causing a convoy of NATO trucks to queue at the closed border.[[13]](#cite_note-14) This convoy was attacked by insurgents, who destroyed more than 29 oil tankers and trucks, and killed several soldiers.[[14]](#cite_note-15) A probe later found that the Americans had mistakenly concluded that the Pakistani soldiers, who had fired warning shots at the helicopters, were insurgents. That led to apologies from the coalition forces in Afghanistan and top NATO and U.S. officials.[[12]](#cite_note-:1-13)

In August 2011, the activity at the Khyber pass was again halted by the [Khyber Agency](/wiki/Khyber_Agency "Khyber Agency") administration due to the possibility of more attacks on NATO forces.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

A number of locations around the world have been named after the Khyber Pass:

* A steep and twisting minor road in [Mugdock Country Park](/wiki/Mugdock_Country_Park "Mugdock Country Park") near [Glasgow](/wiki/Glasgow "Glasgow"), [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"). The road is a landmark along the [West Highland Way](/wiki/West_Highland_Way "West Highland Way") and is popular among local road cyclists.[[16]](#cite_note-17)
* A suburb of [Civil Lines, Delhi](/wiki/Civil_Lines,_Delhi "Civil Lines, Delhi"), India.[[17]](#cite_note-18)[[18]](#cite_note-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20)
* Khyber Pass Road, a major road in the suburb of [Newmarket](/wiki/Newmarket,_New_Zealand "Newmarket, New Zealand"), [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"), New Zealand ([Google Earth view](https://www.google.com/maps/place/Khyber+Pass+Rd,+Auckland,+New+Zealand/@-36.8660807,174.769711,967m/data=!3m1!1e3!4m5!3m4!1s0x6d0d47dec8819525:0x25b0e169619e11c4!8m2!3d-36.8658339!4d174.7695836?hl=en))
* An artificial rockwork feature at [East Park, Kingston upon Hull](/wiki/East_Park,_Kingston_upon_Hull "East Park, Kingston upon Hull"), UK.[[20]](#cite_note-21)[[21]](#cite_note-22)
* Khyber Road in [Phoenix Park](/wiki/Phoenix_Park "Phoenix Park"), [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin"), Ireland.[[22]](#cite_note-The_Irish_Times-23)
* A steep and twisting road up the West Cliff at [Whitby](/wiki/Whitby "Whitby"), UK.
* A pedestrian alley in [Stromness](/wiki/Stromness "Stromness"), Orkney, Scotland[[23]](#cite_note-24)
* Khyber Pass Pub in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), Pennsylvania.
* [The Khyber Himalayan Resort & Spa](/wiki/The_Khyber_Himalayan_Resort_%26_Spa "The Khyber Himalayan Resort & Spa") in [Gulmarg](/wiki/Gulmarg "Gulmarg"), [Jammu and Kashmir](/wiki/Jammu_and_Kashmir_(union_territory) "Jammu and Kashmir (union territory)").
* A mountain bike trail connecting the Top of the World trail at [Whistler, British Columbia](/wiki/Whistler,_British_Columbia "Whistler, British Columbia") to the Whistler Creekside Village.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* A [subway](/wiki/Subway_(underpass) "Subway (underpass)") in the [King's Cross St Pancras tube station](/wiki/King%27s_Cross_St_Pancras_tube_station "King's Cross St Pancras tube station"). After the [King's Cross fire](/wiki/King%27s_Cross_fire "King's Cross fire") in November 1987, it was replaced.[[24]](#cite_note-25)

Other references include the following:

* The bus journey on this road was belle-lettered very beautifully, and a part of its first act, in the selective memoir *[Deshe Bideshe](/wiki/Deshe_Bideshe "Deshe Bideshe")* (1948) by [Syed Mujtaba Ali](/wiki/Syed_Mujtaba_Ali "Syed Mujtaba Ali").
* Before the [partition of India](/wiki/Partition_of_India "Partition of India"), the pass was mentioned as part of common [Hindustani](/wiki/Hindustani_language "Hindustani language") phrase used to describe the length of [colonial India](/wiki/Colonial_India "Colonial India"), "Khyber sé Kanyakumari".[[8]](#cite_note-Rajghatta2017-9)
* 'Khyber Pass' is [Cockney rhyming slang](/wiki/Cockney_rhyming_slang "Cockney rhyming slang") meaning 'arse'. This use is alluded to in the 1968 film *[Carry On Up the Khyber](/wiki/Carry_On_Up_the_Khyber "Carry On Up the Khyber")*.
* In the 1975 movie [*The Man Who Would Be King*](/wiki/The_Man_Who_Would_Be_King_(film) "The Man Who Would Be King (film)"), the character Peachy Carnehan tells [Rudyard Kipling](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling") how he and his comrade-in-arms Danny Dravot had fought under [Frederick Roberts, 1st Earl Roberts](/wiki/Frederick_Roberts,_1st_Earl_Roberts "Frederick Roberts, 1st Earl Roberts") yard by yard through the Khyber Pass during the [Second Anglo-Afghan War](/wiki/Second_Anglo-Afghan_War "Second Anglo-Afghan War") of 1878-1880
* The podcast *Twilight Histories* has an episode called "Napoleon in Afghanistan" which partly takes place in the Khyber Pass.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* The [Vampire Weekend](/wiki/Vampire_Weekend "Vampire Weekend") song "M79" references the Khyber Pass.
* The [Tom Cochrane](/wiki/Tom_Cochrane "Tom Cochrane") song "[Life Is a Highway](/wiki/Life_Is_a_Highway "Life Is a Highway")" (covered by [Rascal Flatts](/wiki/Rascal_Flatts "Rascal Flatts") and others) references the Khyber Pass.
* The album *[Rio Grande Blood](/wiki/Rio_Grande_Blood "Rio Grande Blood")* by [Ministry](/wiki/Ministry_(band) "Ministry (band)") (2006) has a song called "Khyber Pass" which references it as a possible hiding place for then missing and at large [Osama bin Laden](/wiki/Osama_bin_Laden "Osama bin Laden"). This song was also featured at the end of the film *[The Hurt Locker](/wiki/The_Hurt_Locker "The Hurt Locker")*.
* The song "Red War" by [Probot](/wiki/Probot "Probot"), featuring [Max Cavalera](/wiki/Max_Cavalera "Max Cavalera") on vocals, mentions the pass.
* British rock band [Pink Floyd](/wiki/Pink_Floyd "Pink Floyd") references the Khyber in their song "[Up the Khyber](/wiki/Up_the_Khyber "Up the Khyber")", featured on the soundtrack to the film *[More](/wiki/More_(1969_film) "More (1969 film)")*.
* In an episode of the cartoon series *[The World of Commander McBragg](/wiki/The_World_of_Commander_McBragg "The World of Commander McBragg")* titled “Khyber Pass”, the eponymous commander has to fend off ten thousand screaming tribesmen in the Khyber Pass.
* Parts of the 1985 [Jay McInerney](/wiki/Jay_McInerney "Jay McInerney") book *Ransom* take place in or near the Khyber Pass.
* The Khyber pass features in several of [Rudyard Kipling](/wiki/Rudyard_Kipling "Rudyard Kipling")'s poems: it appears by name in "The Ballad of the King's Jest",[[25]](#cite_note-:0-26) as "the Pass" in "Arithmetic on the Frontier",[[25]](#cite_note-:0-26) and semi-fictionalized as the Tongue of [Jagai](/wiki/Shagai_Plateau "Shagai Plateau") in "[The Ballad of East and West](/wiki/The_Ballad_of_East_and_West "The Ballad of East and West")".[[26]](#cite_note-27)
* In the 2023 movie *[Ghosted](/wiki/Ghosted_(2023_film) "Ghosted (2023 film)")*, a farmer played by [Chris Evans](/wiki/Chris_Evans_(actor) "Chris Evans (actor)"), is abducted to the Khyber Pass and rescued by a CIA operative played by [Ana de Armas](/wiki/Ana_de_Armas "Ana de Armas"), as shown by the [location card](/wiki/Intertitle "Intertitle") at 35'15", although the film was actually filmed in [New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico").[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)
* The Belleville boot company has an 8-inch [combat boot](/wiki/Combat_boot "Combat boot") line named Khyber.
